author: @Frankie_7oaks
text:
Rob, I absolutely love your channel, the varied set of subjects that you discuss has really re-invigorated my ageing engineering mind.

One question about this graphene infused plastic, how does it perform thermally (can it take more heat before it falls apart) compared with the same plastic without the infusion?

Would it be possible (ok two questions) to leave the graphene and the plastic crystals in light (loose) suspension within a printable filament which then fully crystalises once the filament is passed through the hot 3D printer head?

That way (assuming that it can withstand greater heat) more complex heat bearing components could be 3D printed, stirling engines, etc…

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: - the glass transition temperature of PEEK is 140 degrees C - you are going to be battling physics about then..    Epoxies can go up to 250C - Glass transition, adding graphene doesn't change underlying material properties.   There are lots of thermal reasons for using metals.

author: @RebelPhoton
text: PEEK is not any kind of ordinary plastic either way. It's one of the highest performance plastics, used in aerospace applications, and very expensive. I'm sure adding graphene improves it but if we can get cheap graphene to improve a cheaper ordinary plastic, like PLA, ABS or Nylon, it becomes even more interesting than the expensive stuff.

replies.text: Reinforced fiber strands in PA6 is your best bet, but hard to print.

replies.text: how about increasing properties in something simple like HMWPE...  aas UHMWPE already produces some of the strongest fibres (PEEK is "just" a lot more temperature resistant, but still being a thermoplastic - not a thermoset - graphene infused high strength epoxy has a future too)

author: @redeyegooner
text:
Hemp plastics and Hempcrete are the future of our survival. Imagine being able to *grow* your own building materials, in only six months, and those building materials not only being as good or better than current materials, they also clean up tons of CO2 from the atmosphere and lock it into the final products, making them better than net zero, actually carbon negative. Also they are biodegradable, or can be crushed after use and used in power stations as clean fuel. Also, the bi products from the refining processes are themselves 100% useable, as fuels, fibres etc. Plus, as it grows, it re-enriches the soli with Nitrogen, making it *better more fertile soil.*
It could litterally change our whole society and planet if we just utilised it instead of condemning it.

replies.author: @ElizabethGreene
replies.text: Peek plastic has a brittle failure mode; it snaps when it fails.  In a vehicle frame you usually want things to bend.  The Graphene might change that, so it's worth testing.

author: @William_Hada
text: Actually master batching your own plastic at home is actually very doable and not very costly. Search for " DIY filament extruder and you will find many options to build your own filament extruder at low cost. I built an extruder from a low cost kit as well as a pelletizer and made some nice filament that I used in my 3d printer. One has the option of running it again through the extrude/pelletize cycle until a good mix is achieved. Also search for " Lyman Filament Extruder " for plans fo build a low cost version.

author: @Opel_Guy
text: When we needed to mix plastic granules and a fine coloured powered dye for injection moulding, we would add a couple of teaspoons of liquid paraffin as a binder to help the dye stick to the granules. This was then tumbled to mix it all together. Worked very well and didn't affect the mouldings. Even worked on a carbon black dye we had which was very fine like icing sugar.

replies.author: @Andykerrfield
replies.text: ​@benholroyd5221 Yep, SynerG, made by a company called Haydale. Prints really really well and I've tested the thermal conductivity with great results.. I wanted to print my own HRU (Heat Recovery Unit) including the plates because cutting a load of aluminium cans to the right size and gluing lollypop sticks for air channels proved to be a bit of a ball ache. The printed graphene plate was lighter, stronger and conducted heat way better than the ally, plus they all fit perfectly in the printed housing chamber ☺️

text: Interesting, and endless possibilities for using that stuff. Would like to see a range of destructive tests, could be a great building material or strengthening for aircraft fuselage and wings.

author: @zipperdeedooha
text: but will it degrade - ever?

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: The base material eventally will. The graphene can be degraded slowly by biology. Humans have at least one enzyme used in immune cells that is capable of breaking a carbon-carbon double bond. Other creatures probably have similar enzymes. It would be difficult to estimate the "half-life" of such graphene doped parts in nature. It's certainly finite, but may literally be on geological timescales.

author: @Tempestan
text: Now to find a way to 3D print it, and the possibilities are endless.

replies.author: @drwoodard72
replies.text: Ya, can that be extruded into a working 3d printer filament?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
​​​​@drwoodard72 Robert said "we had it extruded into standard dog bones for testing". That means it was heated and then extruded into a standard mold that is used for testing.

There is a good chance that would be workable into a filament. But even if its strong in one direction, 3d printed layers tend to be weak in between layers.

replies.author: @jonbutcher9805
replies.text: The heating may have added to it's strength properties.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text:
Once mixed properly, it can be extruded into 3d printer filament, and should have no issues printing. The two problems that are likely to arise are that the layer adhesion strength probably won't be anywhere near the strength along the length of a printed layer, and there's a high likelihood that it would be a very abrassive filament, so nozzles will have to be replaced frequently to maintain quality of prints.
The former can be mostly designed around, but the latter issue is one that will need either money poured into it, or will require exotic nozzle materials like sapphire or diamond to be a non-issue.
